data.title: Gold surges as weak U.S. jobs data raises rate cut hopes
data.description:
Gold futures surged Friday after weaker than expected U.S. payrolls came in softer than expected in July, boosting expectations for an interest rate cut by the Federal Reserve.

U.S. job growth slowed more than expected last month, with non-farm payrolls adding just 73,000 jobs, compared to forecasts of ~100,000 jobs, after rising by a downwardly revised 14,000 in June, and the unemployment rate ticked higher to 4.2% from 4.1% in June, the Labor Department's Bureau of Labor Statistics said.

Even more suprising, revisions to May and June data revised jobs down by a huge 258,000, indicating the jobs market has lost momentum even earlier than expected and certainly will intensify pressure from President Trump on the Fed to loosen monetary policy.

Earlier this week, the Fed left interest rates unchanged in the 4.25%-4.50% range, with Chair Jerome Powell saying "we haven't made any decisions about September."

"We've got a situation where we have inflationary pressures continuing from tariffs and wages, yet job numbers are disappointing. So in that situation, if the Fed cuts [rates], that's going to have a material impact on gold in a positive way," TD Securities head of commodity strategies Bart Melek said in a note.

The marketplace now sees a 75% chance for a Fed rate cut at the September FOMC meeting.

Also supporting gold, President Trump unveiled a batch of new tariffs on exports from dozens of trading partners, including Canada, Brazil, India and Taiwan, which sent global markets tumbling.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for August delivery closed +1.6% to $3,347.70/oz, its highest settlement since July 24, and front-month Comex August silver (XAGUSD:CUR) ended +0.6% to $36.787/oz; for the week, gold gained 0.3% while silver slipped 3.7%.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (NYSEARCA:IAU), (NYSEARCA:NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Cboe raises 2025 organic net revenue growth guidance to high single digits amid record $587M quarter
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cboe Global Markets, Inc. (CBOE) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Craig Donohue, CEO, highlighted Cboe's record-setting quarter, stating that net revenue grew 14% year-over-year to $587 million and adjusted diluted EPS increased 14% to $2.46. He cited broad-based strength, noting, “All 3 categories, Derivative Markets, Cash and Spot Markets and Data Vantage produced double-digit net revenue growth on a year-over-year basis contributing to record first half results for the firm.”
  • The Derivatives franchise set a record with 17% organic net revenue growth and strong volumes across proprietary and multi-list index options. SPX options volumes rose 21% year-over-year to an average daily volume of 3.7 million contracts, while SPX 0DTE options represented a record 57% of overall SPX options volume.
  • Donohue announced the closure of the Japan equities business, reallocating resources to support Japanese customers through derivatives and market data, aligning with Cboe’s strategy to focus on the highest return activities.
  • The Data Vantage business saw net revenue improve by 11% year-over-year, with 45% of new data sales occurring outside the U.S. Cboe is accelerating derivatives data migration to the cloud and expanding its Asia Pacific sales leadership.
  • “Adjusted operating expenses of $213 million were up 8% on a year-over-year basis. Adjusted operating EBITDA of $387 million grew 19% and adjusted operating EBITDA margin expanded by 2.3 percentage points to 65.8% versus the second quarter of 2024,” stated CFO Jill Griebenow.
  • Griebenow mentioned a decrease in full-year expense guidance and outlined anticipated savings from the Japan exit, with an expected pretax charge of $5 million in Q3 2025, and annualized cost savings of $10 million to $12 million.

Outlook

  • Cboe raised its full-year total organic net revenue growth guidance to high single digits from mid- to high single digits, based on strong first-half results.
  • The Data Vantage organic net revenue growth range was reaffirmed at mid- to high single digits.
  • CapEx guidance remains unchanged at $75 million to $85 million; depreciation and amortization expectations lowered to $53 million to $57 million.
  • Cboe expects the effective tax rate on adjusted earnings to be 28.5% to 30.5% for the full year.
  • Guidance for adjusted operating expenses is now $832 million to $847 million, lowered from the previous range.

Financial Results

  • Net revenue reached a record $587 million, with Derivatives Markets net revenues up 17%, Data Vantage up 11%, and Cash and Spot Markets up 11% year-over-year.
  • Adjusted diluted EPS for the quarter was $2.46, up 14% year-over-year.
  • Options ADV increased 20%, while SPX options ADV reached 3.7 million contracts.
  • Europe and APAC segment delivered a 30% net revenue increase, driven by a 39% increase in net transaction and clearing fees.
  • Adjusted operating EBITDA margin was 65.8%.
  • Share repurchases totaled $35 million in the quarter, with $66 million returned to shareholders via dividends.

Q&A

  • Patrick Moley, Piper Sandler: Asked about CEO priorities and M&A strategy. Donohue responded, “I'm going to continue to work with the team to be very focused on optimizing growth in our core and leaning into the secular trends… Anything that we choose to do is going to be something that has to be compelling from both a strategic and a financial rationale perspective.”
  • Dan Fannon, Jefferies: Inquired about further business optimization. Donohue stated, “I'm working with the team to look at everything that we're doing. We're doing that in a rigorous way… the end goal is to just make sure that we're allocating all of our capital... to taking advantage of our best growth opportunities.”
  • Ben Budish, Barclays: Asked about the Data Vantage revenue model and guidance. Catherine Clay replied, “We remain confident in our full year guidance, just recognizing that quarterly results will fluctuate… but we are on track to deliver against our full year objectives.”
  • Elias Abboud, BofA: Asked about long-term index options growth. Clay explained that retail expansion and Asia Pacific demand suggest “this is early innings to sustain the 0DTE complex.”
  • Ashish Sabadra, RBC: Queried on the competitive impact of single stock 0DTE. Clay noted, “We actually see the single stack 0DTEs as not cannibalistic to the index option 0DTE… we see a lot of retail traders coming into the single fact or the ETF options and then migrating into the index option space as that level of sophistication... grows.”
  • Alex Kramm, UBS: Raised the S&P Global index relationship. Donohue called S&P a “long-term and deeply valued partner,” emphasizing a commitment to a mutually beneficial, innovative relationship.
  • Owen Lau, Oppenheimer: Asked about globalization. Chris Isaacson highlighted data sales and cloud distribution, stating, “Where we see greatest opportunities globally is to continue to -- through the import export discussion, really importing flow from outside the U.S. into our markets.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a positive to neutral tone, pressing for details on growth drivers, M&A strategy, and the sustainability of revenue guidance.
  • Management maintained confident and measured language in prepared remarks and Q&A, with Donohue and Clay both emphasizing discipline and rigorous evaluation. Phrases such as “we remain positive” and “we are confident” indicated a steady outlook.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from transition and succession to a focus on operational rigor and strategic optimization, while analysts maintained a consistent balance of curiosity and cautious optimism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The most notable shift was the transition to new CEO Craig Donohue, with an explicit focus on optimizing capital allocation and closing the Japan equities business—actions not announced in the previous quarter.
  • Revenue and EPS growth rates remained robust, but expense guidance was lowered, reflecting improved cost discipline.
  • The company increased its full-year organic net revenue growth guidance, up from mid- to high single digits in Q1 to high single digits in Q2.
  • Management’s tone was more decisive regarding portfolio optimization and capital allocation, contrasting with the prior quarter’s focus on transition and continuity.
  • Analyst questions shifted toward probing the sustainability of Data Vantage growth and the strategic rationale behind business exits.

Risks and Concerns

  • Potential impacts from the closure of the Japan equities business, including a $5 million pretax charge in Q3 2025, and the redirection of resources to higher-return areas.
  • Future expense variability related to higher incentive compensation and planned increases in marketing spend.
  • Uncertainty regarding the pace of international expansion and the evolving competitive landscape in 0DTE options.
  • Management cited ongoing rigorous review of business lines to ensure capital is directed toward the highest value opportunities.

Final Takeaway

Cboe Global Markets delivered record net revenue and EPS in Q2 2025, driven by robust growth across derivatives, data, and cash markets. The company raised its full-year organic net revenue growth guidance, reduced expense outlook, and executed a strategic exit from Japan equities to focus on higher-return opportunities. Management’s focus on disciplined capital allocation, broad-based product strength, and international expansion positions Cboe for continued growth through the second half of 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tesla to appeal Miami decision finding company 33% guilty in fatal Florida autopilot crash - update
data.description:
Updated 17:15ET: with statement from Tesla regarding verdict and decision to appeal. 

A Florida jury has found Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) partly responsible for a fatal 2019 crash, claiming flaws in the company’s autopilot feature were to blame for the accident.

This was the first federal lawsuit involving an accident tied to autopilot to go to trial. The verdict found Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) 33% responsible for the crash and the driver of the Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA), George Brian McGee, 66% responsible. As a result, Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) will only have to pay a third of the $200M awarded in punitive damages and $129M in compensatory damages to the two individuals struck by McGee.

The company was sued by the family of 22-year-old college student Naibel Benavides, a pedestrian struck by the Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA). According to details submitted during the Miami trial, McGee reached for his dropped cell phone with Tesla’s (TSLA) autopilot activated while he drove through an intersection in Key Largo, Florida at 50 mph, crashing into a parked SUV on the side of the road. Benavides and her boyfriend, Dillon Angulo were standing next to the SUV when it was struck by the Tesla (TSLA), killing Benavides and injuring Angulo.

During the trial, McGee said his foot was on the accelerator while crossing the intersection, thereby overriding a function of autopilot that would stop for objects in the road. Attorneys for the plaintiff claim Tesla’s (TSLA) misinformation about the capabilities of autopilot created a false sense of security for the driver. Video from the vehicle shows that autopilot recognized the SUV but did nothing to avoid it. Tesla (TSLA) lawyers argued, however, that McGee was a “reckless” and “aggressive” driver going well over the posted speed limit, citing warnings from the Tesla’s (TSLA) car manual that it is “the drivers’ responsibility to stay alert, drive safely, and be in control of the vehicle,” all of which McGee disregarded.

“No car would have prevented the crash,” Tesla’s (TSLA) attorney Joel Smith said to the jury.

“Today’s verdict is wrong and only works to set back automotive safety and jeopardize Tesla’s and the entire industry’s efforts to develop and implement life-saving technology. We plan to appeal given the substantial errors of law and irregularities at trial. Even though this jury found that the driver was overwhelmingly responsible for this tragic accident in 2019, the evidence has always shown that this driver was solely at fault because he was speeding, with his foot on the accelerator – which overrode Autopilot – as he rummaged for his dropped phone without his eyes on the road. To be clear, no car in 2019, and none today, would have prevented this crash. This was never about Autopilot; it was a fiction concocted by plaintiffs’ lawyers blaming the car when the driver – from day one – admitted and accepted responsibility," Tesla said in a statement to Seeking Alpha.

data.title: RGA signals return to share repurchases and targets $160M–$170M in Equitable transaction income for 2026 amid capital flexibility
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Reinsurance Group of America, Incorporated (RGA) (RGA) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Tony Cheng, President and CEO, reported operating EPS of $4.72 per share and stated "our adjusted operating return on equity for the trailing 12 months, excluding notable items, was 14.3%, which is in line with our intermediate-term targets." Cheng described the quarter as below expectations due to claims volatility in U.S. individual life and unfavorable claims in the healthcare excess business but affirmed "for the year, we are in line with expectations, and our forward-looking views have not changed."
  • Cheng announced the closing of the Equitable transaction with an effective date of April 1 and highlighted a significant increase in excess and deployable capital, noting "this will give us considerably more flexibility going forward to fund not only our strong growth but also return capital to shareholders in the form of dividends and share repurchases."
  • Cheng emphasized global expansion, citing asset-intensive wins across five countries and robust 11% premium growth in traditional business on a constant currency basis for the first half of the year. He stated, "our focus on Creation Re... allows us to continue to exceed our targets in terms of the percentage of business coming from exclusive arrangements."
  • Cheng added, "I am delighted with the closing of the Equitable transaction, as we announced yesterday... the experience on the block in Q2 was in line with our expectations."
  • Axel Andre, CFO, stated, "RGA reported pretax adjusted operating income of $421 million for the quarter or $4.72 per share after tax... our excess capital increased to $3.8 billion at the end of Q2. Pro forma for the Equitable transaction... excess capital was $2.3 billion."

Outlook

  • Cheng stated, "we remain confident about the future of our business prospects as RGA is well positioned in its markets, and we have a proven successful strategy that has stood the test of time."
  • Andre provided forward guidance: "For the second half of 2025, we still expect pretax operating income contributions of approximately $70 million, increasing to $160 million to $170 million in 2026 and approximately $200 million per year by 2027" from the Equitable transaction.
  • Management expects group business overall to be "approximately breakeven" for the second half of the year versus a prior expectation of $20 million to $30 million.
  • The effective tax rate for the full year is expected to remain 23% to 24%.

Financial Results

  • Cheng reported "operating results were below expectations due to large claims volatility in U.S. individual life and unfavorable claims in our healthcare excess business."
  • Andre stated, "claims experience was unfavorable in the quarter, primarily driven by the U.S. Traditional segment... economic claims experience was lower than expected by $256 million with a corresponding $158 million unfavorable current period financial impact."
  • Consolidated net premiums were up 14% year-over-year when adjusted for U.S. PRT transactions; traditional business premium growth was 11% year-to-date on a constant currency basis.
  • Andre reported, "total variable investment income was strong at $105 million, significantly higher than last quarter and now favorable for the year."
  • Book value per share, excluding AOCI and B36 embedded derivatives, increased to $156.63.
  • Quarterly dividend was increased 4.5% to $0.93 per share.

Q&A

  • John Bakewell Barnidge, Piper Sandler: Asked about additional credit on the life block and actuarial assumptions. Cheng responded the credit reflects "the current book of business with current assumptions and does not reflect any change in our actuarial assumptions at this point."
  • Joel Robert Hurwitz, Dowling & Partners: Inquired about individual life experience lag effects and retentions. Jonathan Porter explained, "the total number of large claims we get in any one quarter is less than 200... a small change in count or average size can create fluctuations in the experience, and that's really what we saw in Q2."
  • Elyse Beth Greenspan, Wells Fargo: Asked about healthcare experience and rate increases. Porter said, "the results this quarter are really driven by higher claims costs stemming from a variety of more expensive treatments... we have already implemented rate increases so far this year... those are significant."
  • Jamminder Singh Bhullar, JPMorgan: Queried on excess capital use and share repurchases. Cheng noted, "we have not bought back stock for the past 6 quarters. So it is important communication that we will commence considering buying back stock from this point forward."
  • Other analysts focused on the value of in-force credit recognition, capital deployment, and the impact of medical advances on long-term mortality trends.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative, pressing on claims volatility, capital deployment, and the defensibility of assumptions. Persistent questions targeted the sustainability of new capital credits and the timing of share repurchases.
  • Management exhibited confidence in strategic direction and capital position but was more defensive when addressing volatility and short-term earnings headwinds. Cheng stated, "the market is going to do what the market's going to do... our job is to continue to grow the EPS and continue to raise the ROE."
  • Compared to last quarter, management’s tone became more defensive amid questions on volatility, while analysts’ concerns increased regarding earnings predictability and capital return actions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from high confidence after strong Q1 results to a more cautious stance, particularly on claims volatility and healthcare excess losses.
  • Management introduced the intention to resume share repurchases, a notable development absent in Q1.
  • Key metrics such as operating EPS and ROE declined sequentially, with operating EPS falling from $5.66 in Q1 to $4.72 in Q2 and ROE moving from 15% to 14.3%.
  • Analysts in Q2 focused more on volatility, capital deployment, and the credibility of in-force credit recognition, compared to pipeline and competitive dynamics in Q1.
  • Management’s confidence in long-term strategy was consistent, but the tone became more cautious regarding near-term results.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited claims volatility in U.S. individual life and unfavorable healthcare excess claims as primary headwinds.
  • The healthcare excess business will be repriced by January 2026, with already implemented significant rate increases.
  • Analysts raised concerns about volatility, the sustainability of capital credits, and the timing and scale of share repurchases.
  • Management emphasized risk discipline and indicated ongoing review of actuarial assumptions, with no immediate changes planned.

Final Takeaway

RGA management highlighted a quarter of strategic progress, including the closing of the Equitable transaction, increased capital flexibility, and renewed focus on returning capital to shareholders through both dividends and share repurchases. While claims volatility and healthcare excess losses pressured short-term results, management reiterated confidence in long-term targets and the durability of its global platform. The company expects improved operating income from the Equitable transaction in coming years and signaled opportunistic share repurchases ahead, supported by significant excess capital and ongoing business momentum.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Back to the corner office: Jim Koch returns as CEO of Boston Beer
data.description:
Jim Koch is returning as CEO of Boston Beer (NYSE:SAM), the company he founded over four decades ago, following the resignation of Michael Spillane after just 16 months in the role.

Spillane stepped down for personal reasons, effective August 15, at which point Koch will take over while continuing to serve as chairman.

“[Spillane] has given us a steady leadership hand during a dynamic time for our industry, and under his guidance, we delivered one of our most profitable quarters in many years in the second quarter,” Koch said in a statement. During his tenure at the helm, Spillane increased focus on new brands including Sun Cruiser and adjusted advertising strategies to a younger audience.

Spillane will remain with the company as a non-executive member of the board of directors.

Koch started Sam Adams in his kitchen by brewing his German great-great-grandfather’s beer recipe he found in his parent’s attic. Before going public in 1995 as The Boston Beer Company (NYSE:SAM), Sam Adams was earning around $200M, eventually reaching a valuation of as much as $12.2B in 2020. The company is currently worth a little over $2.2B.  

Over the past several years, Boston Beer (NYSE:SAM) has struggled with changing consumer tastes, weight loss medications, and tariffs, the latter of which the company estimated would cost $15M to $20M in FY25.

Additionally, the company’s addition of hard seltzers and teas continues to weigh on profitability, with category sales down another 7% during the second quarter of this year.

“Our depletions declined by 5% in the second quarter as volumes were pressured across the beer industry due to economic uncertainty impacting consumer behavior and some impact from poor weather in some key selling weeks,” Koch said in the company’s most recent quarterly report.

But while the macroeconomic environment remains challenging, the company’s first half performance and strong operating plans for the remainder of the summer “gives us confidence in the ability to deliver full year financial guidance,” CEO Michael Spillane added.

With Koch returning to the helm, Boston Beer (SAM) is betting on its founder's experience and long-standing vision to help navigate a rapidly evolving landscape.

data.title: Full Truck Alliance announces changes to its freight brokerage service
data.description:
• Full Truck Alliance (NYSE:YMM) announced on Friday changes to its freight brokerage service.
  • To ensure sustainability of its freight brokerage service, the company has decided to increase the service fee rate for freight brokerage service to reduce such service's reliance on government grants and potential uncertainties. 
  • The company understands that such changes may increase costs to shippers. 
  • The company expects that, starting from the quarter ending September 30, 2025, the transaction volume of its freight brokerage service will significantly decline, resulting in a decline in revenue from freight brokerage service, while the cost of revenue for such service will increase, which may adversely affect the company's profit to certain extent.

data.title: Tech Voices: Palantir's $10B contract, Tesla verdict, Airbnb (Update)
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements and remarks that could impact the technology sector.

  • The U.S. Army said it has signed a new Enterprise Agreement with Palantir worth up to $10 billion that "establishes a comprehensive framework for the Army's future software and data needs." 

The new EA will consolidate 75 contracts into a single contract, which will help reduce procurement time and "allow the government flexibility to purchase goods and services as needed," the Army said in a statement.

"This Enterprise Agreement represents a pivotal step in the Army's commitment to modernizing our capabilities while being fiscally responsible," said U.S. Army CIO Leo Garciga in the statement. "By streamlining our procurement processes and leveraging enterprise-level discounts, we are not only enhancing our operational effectiveness but also maximizing our buying power."

  • A Miami jury has found Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) 33% liable for a fatal car crash in 2019 that involved one of its Autopilot-equipped vehicles, while awarding $329 million in damages to a crash survivor and the deceased victim's family.

The accident occurred after the Tesla driver took his eyes off the road to pick up his cellphone, believing that the car's Enhanced Autopilot system would brake if it sensed any obstacles in the way. Instead, the car accelerated and hit two people on the side of the road, killing one, according to CNBC.

“Tesla designed Autopilot only for controlled access highways yet deliberately chose not to restrict drivers from using it elsewhere, alongside Elon Musk telling the world Autopilot drove better than humans,” the plaintiff's counsel, Brett Schreiber, told CNBC in an e-mailed statement on Friday. “Tesla’s lies turned our roads into test tracks for their fundamentally flawed technology, putting everyday Americans like Naibel Benavides and Dillon Angulo in harm’s way.”

The plaintiffs' attorneys said they expect Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) to pay around $242.5 million of the damages awarded. Tesla plans to appeal the decision, CNBC added.

  • Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) is optimistic that restrictions on its service in New York City will be lifted.

“Conversations are happening there, top-level conversations with the mayor and with mayoral candidates,” Airbnb CBO Dave Stephenson said in an interview with Bloomberg. “We’re having really good conversations about the importance of supporting the communities and the economics in those communities and I think that they’re realizing that the law is not serving the community well.”

Bloomberg noted that the company plans to spend around $10 million this year on local and state races to get politicians sympathetic to its cause elected. The company has already spent over $1 million on the New York City mayoral race.

(Adds details about Tesla judgment.)

data.title: Linde outlines $16.30–$16.50 full-year EPS target as project backlog and cash flow hit record levels
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Linde plc (LIN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Sanjiv Lamba highlighted that "EPS of $4.09 and operating margin of 30.1%, both represent all-time quarterly highs, against the backdrop of a challenging macro environment." He noted operating cash flows grew 15% and ROC reached 25.1%. Lamba focused on the project backlog, stating, "In a little over 4 years, the sale of gas backlog has approximately doubled, from $3.6 billion to $7.1 billion," emphasizing that almost three-quarters of this backlog is in the Americas, mainly for electronics and clean energy projects.
  • Lamba announced the addition of the Blue Point project, a joint venture in Louisiana, as a significant clean energy contract, bringing the total to approximately $5 billion in large clean energy wins. He also discussed a new merchant investment in the Southeastern U.S. supporting space launches and mentioned that tuck-in acquisitions contributed a 1% top line increase in the quarter.
  • CFO Matthew J. White stated, "Sales of $8.5 billion increased 3% over prior year and 5% sequentially. Year-over-year FX headwinds abated as we saw a 3% sequential improvement from broad-based weakening of the U.S. dollar." White also noted, "Operating profit of $2.6 billion increased 6% over prior year. The operating margin of 30.1% increased 80 basis points or 100 basis points when excluding the effect of cost pass-through. EPS of $4.09 also increased 6% from prior year as a lower share count was mostly offset by a higher effective tax rate."

Outlook

  • White provided Q3 guidance: "For the third quarter, we're providing a guidance range of $4.10 to $4.20, or 4% to 7% above last year. This includes an assumed 1% currency tailwind, which would be the first quarterly FX benefit since late 2023."
  • For the full year, White said, "This resulted in a new range of $16.30 to $16.50, or 5% to 6% growth, including a 1% currency tailwind."
  • White explained the guidance approach as updating improved FX but offsetting with an assumption of economic contraction at the top end.

Financial Results

  • Linde reported sales of $8.5 billion, operating profit of $2.6 billion, and operating cash flow growth of 15% for the quarter. Operating margin reached 30.1%, and EPS was $4.09.
  • Acquisitions lifted sales 1% over prior year, and underlying sales grew 1% over prior year and 3% sequentially. Volumes were down 1% from last year, mainly due to weaker base volumes in EMEA.
  • White stated, "The pie chart represents a steady and disciplined capital allocation policy, which deployed almost $6.5 billion year-to-date. Of this, $2.8 billion comprises investments that met our risk-reward criteria, an increase of 20% over last year."
  • Linde issued CHF 0.5 billion in bonds with an average yield less than 1% during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Patrick Duffy Fischer, Goldman Sachs, asked about geographic and end market outlook. Lamba responded that in the Americas, "growth in the resilient end market" offsets a softer industrial sector and expressed confidence in the U.S. market, whereas Europe is expected to see continued softening with "no catalyst for economic improvement this year." Asia remains mixed, with India as a bright spot.
  • David L. Begleiter, Deutsche Bank, inquired about pricing risks. Lamba stated, "over the last 25 years, Praxair-Linde has always achieved positive pricing... and that remains the expectation going forward as well."
  • Vincent Andrews, Morgan Stanley, questioned margin dynamics. White noted, "almost 32% margins we're tracking. We clearly see more room to improve."
  • Laurent Favre, BNP Paribas, asked about project backlog risks. Lamba reiterated, "my expectation remains that we will end the year with a backlog with a 7 handle on it..."
  • Jeffrey John Zekauskas, JPMorgan, questioned EBIT growth and helium impact. White attributed EMEA EBIT growth to FX, pricing, and productivity, and Lamba noted, "our exposure on helium is very different and much smaller... year-to-date, our helium volumes are flat."
  • Other questions addressed European volume risks, growth in the commercial space sector, return profiles of backlog projects, electronics, energy transition, base volume guidance, and the impact of U.S. tax policy changes.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a cautious but constructive tone, frequently asking about geographic volume trends, pricing sustainability, and backlog risks. They pressed for specifics on European and Chinese outlooks, margin sustainability, and project execution amid macro uncertainty.
  • Management displayed confidence in their pricing power, backlog turnover, and capital allocation. Phrases like "I'm highly confident" and "I'm certain Linde will remain the undisputed leader" signaled optimism. However, White tempered expectations, saying, "currency volatility and general economic uncertainty don't give us enough confidence to raise the outlook at this time."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a similarly guarded but proactive tone, while analysts continued to probe for downside risks and execution clarity.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q3 and the full year shifted to include a currency tailwind and an explicit economic contraction assumption at the top end, compared to last quarter's offsetting of improved FX with negative volume assumptions.
  • Project backlog remains robust, while management highlighted increased project turnover and execution. Analysts maintained focus on geographic weakness, especially Europe, and the sustainability of margin and pricing gains.
  • Management's confidence in U.S. market growth and space sector opportunities increased, with more discussion on base CapEx and tuck-in acquisitions. Analysts' skepticism about Europe and project timing persisted.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Lamba described Europe as facing "several challenges to get their economy back on their feet" and noted no current catalyst for improvement. He acknowledged the risk of further volume declines in the region.
  • Management cited economic uncertainty, currency volatility, and weak industrial demand as ongoing headwinds. Lamba highlighted that the negative volume headwinds are "a function of contractual customers taking less gas due to the economic uncertainty."
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned potential delays or slippage in project backlog and the risk to future growth in Europe and China.

Final Takeaway

Linde's Q2 2025 call emphasized record project backlog, strong cash flow, and robust EPS and margin performance despite a challenging global environment. Management reaffirmed its disciplined approach to project investment, pricing, and capital allocation, signaling confidence in capturing growth from resilient end markets, U.S. and APAC expansion, and emerging sectors such as commercial space. While full-year EPS guidance was raised to $16.30–$16.50, the company remains cautious about macroeconomic conditions, particularly in Europe, but continues to expect its operational resilience and project execution to drive long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
